coredata.dc:title: L-cystine-linked BODIPY-adsorbed monolayer MoS2 quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of biothiols based on the inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Krishna Kumar, A. Santhana
Tseng, Wei-Bin
Wu, Man-Jyun
Huang, Yzu-Yun
Tseng, Wei-Lung
coredata.dc:description: This study fabricated a dual-emission probe consisting of monolayer MoS2 quantum dots (M − MoS2 QDs) and L-cystine-linked boron-dipyrromethene (L-Cys-BODIPY) molecules for ratiometric sensing of biothiols, thiol product-related enzyme reactions, and ratiometric imaging of glutathione (GSH)-related reactions in HeLa cells. The formation of L-Cys-BODIPY-adsorbed M − MoS2 QDs (named as BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs) was demonstrated by comparing them with M − MoS2 QDs using transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs exhibited dual-emission bands, excellent biocompatibility, and good resistance to photobleaching. It was found that the adsorbed L-Cys-BODIPY molecules rarely quenched the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs, and meanwhile, they were self-quenched by π-π stacking between each BODIPY backbones. The presence of biothiols induced the reduction of weakly fluorescent L-Cys-BODIPY to strongly fluorescent of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. Since having a much higher molar absorption coefficient than L-Cys-BODIPY, the liberated L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY behaved as an effective inner filter to absorb the excitation light and subsequently quenched the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs. The appearance of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY could barely affected to the fluorescence lifetime of M − MoS2 QDs, confirming the inner filter effect of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY onto the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs. The present probe not only provided a linear ratiometric response to 1–10 mM GSH, 1–10 μM cysteine, and 1–10 μM of homocysteine but also remarkably showed the ratiometric detection of thiol products from the reactions of 1–900 units L−1 S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH) hydrolase and SAH as well as 1–850 units L−1 GSH reductase and disulfide GSH. Additionally, the present probe was well-suited for ratiometric imaging of intracellular GSH levels in non-treated and drug-treated HeLa cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparing the morphology of M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs. (A, D) TEM images, (B, E) size distributions, (C, F) high-resolution TEM images, (G, I) AFM images, and (H, J) height profiles of (A, B, C, G, H) the M − MoS2 QDs and (D, E, F, I, J) the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the M − MoS2 QDs as a function of excitation wavelengths. (B) Fluorescence spectra of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs in ultrapure water at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. (C) Fluorescence intensity of FITC molecules (fuchsia triangle), M − MoS2 QDs (black square), and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs (418 nm, red diamond; 518 nm, blue triangle) under continuous irradiation of a 150 W Xe lamp at 365 nm. (D, E, F) The F518 nm/F418 nm value of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs as a function of (D) NaCl concentrations, (E) solution pH, and (F) storage time. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic illustration of mechanism associated with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs for ratiometric detection of thiols: (A) the M − MoS2 QDs emit strong fluorescence at 418 nm when excited at 340 nm; (B) L-Cys-BODIPY molecules are physisorbed on the M − MoS2 QD surface via their disulfide bond and BODIPY moieties and meanwhile, their fluorescence is self-quenched due to π-π stacking between each BODIPY moieties based on the combination of two processes; (C) thiols trigger the reduction of L-Cys-BODIPY to L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY that efficiently quenches the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs via the IFE process.

(A) Absorption spectra of (a) 15 μM L-Cys-BODIPY and (b) 30 μM L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. (B) Fluorescence lifetime decay and (C) fluorescence spectra of M − MoS2 QDs as a function of the concentration of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. (B, C) The arrow indicates an increase in the concentration of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY (0, 10, 20, and 30 μM). The M − MoS2 QDs were mixed with L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4).

(A–C) Fluorescence spectra obtained from the incubation of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs with (A) 0–10 mM GSH, (B) 0–10 μM cysteine, and (C) 0–10 μM homocysteine at ambient temperature in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) for 1 h. The arrow indicates the fluorescence change with an increase in the concentration of biothiols. (D–F) A plot of the value of F518 nm/F418 nm as a function of the concentrations of (D) GSH, (E) cysteine, and (F) homocysteine. The error bars in (D), (E), and (F) were calculated from the standard deviation of three individual measurements.

CLSM imaging of GSH levels in (A, B) untreated, (E, F) L-BSO-treated, and (I, J) GSH-OET-treated HeLa cells stained with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs for 2 h at 37 °C. The merged images of (C), (G), and (K) are the overlap of (A) and (B), (E) and (F), and (I) and (J), in sequence. The mean values of (A, E, I) the blue intensity, (B, F, J) the green intensity, and the Fgreen/Fblue ratio obtained from the measurement (n = 30) of (D) untreated, (H) L-BSO-treated, and (L) GSH-OET-treated HeLa cells. The scale bar is 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: L-cystine-linked BODIPY-adsorbed monolayer MoS2 quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of biothiols based on the inner filter effect

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study fabricated a dual-emission probe consisting of monolayer MoS2 quantum dots (M − MoS2 QDs) and L-cystine-linked boron-dipyrromethene (L-Cys-BODIPY) molecules for ratiometric sensing of biothiols, thiol product-related enzyme reactions, and ratiometric imaging of glutathione (GSH)-related reactions in HeLa cells. The formation of L-Cys-BODIPY-adsorbed M − MoS2 QDs (named as BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs) was demonstrated by comparing them with M − MoS2 QDs using transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs exhibited dual-emission bands, excellent biocompatibility, and good resistance to photobleaching. It was found that the adsorbed L-Cys-BODIPY molecules rarely quenched the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs, and meanwhile, they were self-quenched by π-π stacking between each BODIPY backbones. The presence of biothiols induced the reduction of weakly fluorescent L-Cys-BODIPY to strongly fluorescent of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. Since having a much higher molar absorption coefficient than L-Cys-BODIPY, the liberated L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY behaved as an effective inner filter to absorb the excitation light and subsequently quenched the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs. The appearance of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY could barely affected to the fluorescence lifetime of M − MoS2 QDs, confirming the inner filter effect of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY onto the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs. The present probe not only provided a linear ratiometric response to 1–10 mM GSH, 1–10 μM cysteine, and 1–10 μM of homocysteine but also remarkably showed the ratiometric detection of thiol products from the reactions of 1–900 units L−1 S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH) hydrolase and SAH as well as 1–850 units L−1 GSH reductase and disulfide GSH. Additionally, the present probe was well-suited for ratiometric imaging of intracellular GSH levels in non-treated and drug-treated HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The topic of fluorescent sensing of biothiols has gained tremendous recognition in recent decades due to their pivotal roles in physiological pathways such as detoxification and metabolism [1,2]. Examples of low-molecular-weight biothiols include cysteine, homocysteine, and glutathione (GSH). In comparison to GSH that is profoundly abundant (1–10 mM) in the intracellular environment, the level of cysteine (150–250 μM) in human plasma is much higher than that of the other biothiols [3]. An increase or reduction in the level of biothiols in biological fluids was reported to be connected with a series of diseases. For instance, L-cysteine deficiency is known to be involved with several disorders such as skin lesions, hair decoloration, edema, liver damage, and sluggish development in the child [4]. Moreover, an inappropriate level of GSH is implicated in cancer-related diseases, Alzheimer’s disease, and other ailments [5]. Thiol molecules are also generated by enzyme-catalyzed cleavage of the substrate that is exemplified by S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase (SAHH)-mediated hydrolysis of S-adenosylhomocysteine [6], glutathione reductase (GR)-catalyzed reduction of GSH [7], and acetylcholinesterase-catalyzed hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine [8]. The activity of thiol product-related enzymes might reflect to a specific disease or syndrome [9]. For example, a deficiency of SAHH activity has been identified to cause hypermethioninemia [10]. Consequently, it’s highly necessary to develop a straightforward method for accurate, precision, and selective quantification of biothiols and thiol product-related to enzymatic reactions in biological systems.

In response to this demand, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) have been developed as gold-standard tools for the quantitative determination of biothiols in biological samples [11]. Nevertheless, these two separation methods need a time-consuming and labor-intensive process that facilitates the autoxidation of biothiols during the whole analysis. Additionally, HPLC and CE are both hard to image the distribution of biothiols in live cells. Considering that fluorescence microscopy techniques can provide the information of high spatial and temporal resolution in a living system, extensive research efforts have been devoted to the synthesis of organic fluorophores and nanomaterial-related nano-sensors for the quantification and imaging of biothiols in real-world samples [12–15]. In general, numerous organic fluorophores are rationally designed to probe intracellular biothiols based on the cyclization with aldehydes [16], Michael addition [17], conjugated addition–cyclization [18], metal complex-displacement coordination [19], cleavage reactions [20], and nucleophilic substitution [21]. Recently, fluorophore-conjugated and quencher-modified nanomaterials (e.g., metal nanoparticles, carbon dots, graphene quantum dots, MnO2 nanosheets, and MoS2 quantum dots) have been introduced to be promising materials for sensing and imaging biothiols in live cells [22–27]. For example, 6-mercaptopurine-modified monolayer MoS2 quantum dots (M − MoS2 QDs) were utilized for the fluorescence turn-on detection of GSH in human fluids and live cells based on the GSH-mediated removal of 6-mercaptopurine (i.e., quencher) from the M − MoS2 QDs. Although these probes all provided satisfactory sensitivity and specificity toward intracellular biothiols, most of them only exhibit a single emission band in response to biothiols. In other words, the luminescent signal of a single-emission probe is susceptible to environmental factors (e.g., temperature, solvent polarity, and pH), probe concentration, and excitation power, resulting in significant fluctuation in the signal output [28]. By contrast, a dual-emission probe provides well accurate and more precise quantification of biothiols by simultaneously monitoring the ratio of two separate emission bands instead of measuring the change in fluorescence intensity. Additionally, a dual-emission probe can ratiometrically image the level of intracellular biothiols by simultaneously acquiring dual-wavelength images and then calculating their intensity ratio. In comparison to fluorescence intensity imaging methods, ratiometric imaging offers great opportunity to obtain the reproducible measurement. The dual-emission probes are frequently fabricated by modifying a single emission fluorophore with a thiol-reactive group [29–31], conjugating two kinds of fluorophores [32–35], functionalizing luminescent nanomaterials with fluorophores [36], and integrating by two different types of luminescent nanomaterials [37,38]. However, these existing ratiometric probes are too sensitive to detect a high concentration of intracellular GSH. Besides, no previous studies reported the use of ratiometric sensor for monitoring thiol product-related enzymatic reactions.

In this work, we focused on the development of dual-emission probe for overcoming these problems by the physisorption of L-cystine-linked boron-dipyrromethene (L-Cys-BODIPY) on the surface of monolayer MoS2 quantum dots (M − MoS2 QDs) through their disulfide bonds and BODIPY moieties [39]. Experimental results of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) studies revealed the successful fabrication of L-Cys-BODIPY-adsorbed M − MoS2 QDs (named as BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs). It was found that the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were capable of ratiometrically quantifying 1–10 mM GSH, 1–10 μM cysteine, and 1–10 μM homocysteine through two successive steps: (i) biothiols trigger the reduction of weakly fluorescent L-Cys-BODIPY to brightly fluorescent L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY, and (ii) L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY efficiently absorbs the excitation light, resulting in the inner filter effect (IFE)-mediated fluorescence quenching of M − MoS2 QDs. The BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were also applicable for ratiometrically monitoring GSH-related reactions in HeLa cells and probing thiol product-related enzymatic reactions. In contrast to our previously reported L-cysteine-BODIPY-conjugated WS2 nanosheets with the single-emission band [22], the dual-emission BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs not only offer precise and accurate quantification of biothiols and thiol product-related enzymatic reactions but also exhibit an appropriate linear range for ratiometric sensing of intracellular GSH levels.

MoS2 superfine powder was supplied from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, Massachusetts). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Macron Fine Chemical (Center Valley, PA, USA). GSH, cysteine, homocysteine, cysteamine, amino acids, glutathione reduced ethyl ester (GSH-OET), L-buthionine-sulfoximine (L-BSO) S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH), S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase (SAHH; rabbit erythrocytes; 50,000 units L−1), GSH disulfide, glutathione reductase (500 units L−1), and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Louis, MO, USA). L-Cys-BODIPY was ordered from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). All other chemicals and reagents (analytical-grade) were listed in the supplementary material.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cintra 10e; GBC, Victoria, Australia) and a Hitachi F-7000 fluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips Tecnai F20 G2 FEI-TEM system (Roanoke, VA) operating at 200 kV. The TEM images were taken on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed on a Dimension 3100 scanning probe microscope (Veeco Digital Instruments, Santa Barbara, CA) operating in the tapping mode coupled to a NSG01 probe (NT-MDT Spectrum Instruments). Time-resolved fluorescence analyses were conducted by using a time-correlated single-photon counting system (Time-Harp 200, PicoQuant GmbH, Berlin, Germany) coupled to a pulsed diode laser at 375 nm (tens of picoseconds pulse width). The surface chemistry and elemental composition of nanomaterials were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI QuanteraII, ULVAC-PHI, Inc., Japan) equipped with a monochromatized Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) and a hemispherical analyzer-128-channel detector under a vacuum of 2.0 × 10−7 Pa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal synthesis of M − MoS2 QDs was performed by according to the method described earlier in studies [22,23,40]. DMF (100 mL) was adopted to exfoliate layered MoS2 powder (1 g). The as-prepared solution was ultra-sonicated (150 W; Elmasonic E60H, Elma, Singen, Germany) in an ice bath for 3 h, followed by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 30 °C, and 0.5 h) to remove large-sized MoS2 nanosheets. The resultant supernatant was transferred to the flask, heated up to 145 °C for 8 h, and then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 60 min. The obtained supernatant was transferred into a 20-ml scintillation vial and then dried to a constant weight using a rotary evaporator. The mass concentration of the dried M − MoS2 QDs was estimated to be 2 mg mL−1 by weighing the difference between the empty flask and the dried QDs-containing flask.

For the loading of L-Cys-BODIPY on the M − MoS2 surface, L-Cys-BODIPY was initially dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide as a stock solution and subsequently diluted to the needed concentration with deionized water. After that, L-Cys-BODIPY (50 μM, 20 μL) was mixed with phosphate buffer (20 mM, 100 μL; pH 7.4) under vigorous shaking for 2 min. The resultant solution (120 μL) was incubated with the M − MoS2 QDs (2 mg mL−1, 200 μL) at ambient temperature for 1 h. The formed BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 45 min. After discarding the supernatant, the obtained precipitate corresponding to the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs was ultrasonicated to be dispersed in Milli-Q water at ambient temperature for 10 min [22]. The dispersed BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were stored at 4 °C until further analysis. After the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs dried in an oven, their mass concentration was determined to be 0.3 mg mL−1. The instruments were utilized for the detailed characterization of the M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were described in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The IFE of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY on the M − MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP; 10 mM, 100 μL), a reducing agent, was prepared in 5 mM phosphate buffer (600 μL; pH 7.4). The prepared TCEP solution (700 μL) was incubated with L-Cys-BODIPY (100 μM, 50 μL) at ambient temperature for 1 h, leading to the production of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. Subsequently, we measured the absorption spectra of various concentrations of L-Cys-BODIPY and L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY and then determined their molar absorption coefficients according to Beer’s law. In an effort to examine the IFE process, L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY (0–30 μM, 120 μL) were mixed with the M − MoS2 QDs (2 mg mL−1, 200 μL) at ambient temperature for 1 h. After that, the resultant solution was transferred in to a 1.5 mL quartz cuvette prior to the acquisition of fluorescence spectra and lifetimes.

The ratiometric sensing of biothiols and thiol product-related enzymatic reaction procedure were described in below: (a) Biothiols: Different concentrations of cysteine (100 μL, 1–10 μM), homocysteine (100 μL, 1–10 μM), and GSH (100 μL, 1–10 mM) were separately incubated with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg mL−1) in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at ambient temperature for 1 h. Amino acids were used in place of biothiols to examine the selectivity of the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs. (b) Enzymatic reactions: For sensing of SAHH, (SAH 100 μL, 100 μM) was initially mixed with various concentrations of SAHH (100 μL, 1–900 units L−1). Subsequently, the mixture was incubated with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg mL−1) under the same conditions were described for biothiol sensing. This procedure was also utilized to evaluate the activity of glutathione reductase at a fixed concentration of GSH disulfide (100 μL, 10 mM) with varying the activity of glutathione reductase (100 μL, 1–850 units L−1). The fluorescence spectra of resultant solutions mentioned above were collected at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The quantification of biothiols and thiol products from the enzymatic reactions was conducted by monitoring the fluorescence peaks of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs at 418 nm (F418 nm) and 518 nm (F518 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric assay of GSH in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures for culturing HeLa cells (cervical cancer cell line; Food Industry Research and Development Institute, Hsinchu, Taiwan) and the MTT assay were discussed in the supplementary material. The BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs probe were used for ratiometric imaging of the intracellular GSH-related reactions. Briefly, HeLa cells (approximately 105 cells) were treated with either 10 mM L-BSO or 10 mM GSH-OET in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium that consists of 1% penicillin-streptomycin and 10% fetal bovine serum. The resultant cells were maintained in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, followed by washing with 1x phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The washed cells were stained with BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg mL−1) in the culture media at 37 °C for 2 h. After washing twice with 1x PBS, the labeled cells were treated with 4% paraformaldehyde (buffered in 1x PBS) for 20 min. The fixed cells were rinsed twice with 1x PBS, and their fluorescence images were collected on an LSM700 confocal laser-scanning microscope (CLSM; Carl Zeiss GmbH, Jena, Germany) equipped with two photomultiplier tube detectors, a violet diode laser (405 nm), and an oil-immersion lens (63x). Two emission intensities of the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs in HeLa cells were collected in spectral ranges from 400 to 490 nm (blue channel) and 555–700 nm (green channel). The ImageJ software would allow the generation of ratiometric images by calculating their ratio of emission intensity from green channel to that of the blue channel.

Fig. S1 (supplementary material) illustrates the -mediated exfoliation method and ultra-sonication treatment for the synthesis of M − MoS2 QDs from layered MoS2 powder [23,40]. Previous studies would suggest that, disulfide bonds and benzene rings of BSA can efficiently bind to the surface of MoS2 nanosheets [39,41]. Hence, we reason that L-Cys-BODIPY molecules could be strongly physisorbed onto the surface of M − MoS2 QDs through their disulfide bond along with each BODIPY moieties. In an effort to test this hypothesis, we conducted a comparative analysis of M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs using TEM, AFM, and XPS. The TEM images display that, average lateral sizes of M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were 3.9 ± 0.5 and 5.4 ± 0.2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A, B, 1D, 1E). The d-spacing values of M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were separately found to be 0.27 and 0.20 nm (Fig. 1C and F), respectively, corresponding to the (100) and (006) faces of layered MoS2 powder [23]. As shown in the AFM images, the average heights of M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs were separately estimated to be approximately 1.0 and 1.1 nm (Fig. 1H–J). Since L-Cys-BODIPY molecules were slightly induced by stacking of the M − MoS2 QDs, the lateral size and height profile of M − MoS2 QDs differ from those BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs. XPS studies was implemented to determine the elemental compositions for two different kinds of QDs. A comparison in the XPS peaks between two kinds of QDs indicates that the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs include additional elements of boron and nitrogen, which is attributable to the physisorption of L-Cys-BODIPY molecules on the surface of M − MoS2 QDs (Fig. S2, supplementary material).

These optical properties for two kinds of QDs were comparatively investigated in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). In the absence of L-Cys-BODIPY, the fluorescence peak of M − MoS2 QDs was progressively red-shifted with increasing the excitation wavelength at an interval of 10 nm (Fig. 2 A). This excitation-dependent emission behavior stems from the fact that individual M − MoS2 QDs have different degrees of edge sites and surface imperfection [42]. As excited at 340 nm, the M − MoS2 QDs exhibited an intense fluorescence peak at 418 nm. Once L-Cys-BODIPY molecules were physisorbed on the surface of M − MoS2 QDs, the resultant BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs displayed dual fluorescence peaks at 418 nm (F418 nm) and 518 nm (F518 nm) that separately originate from the excited M − MoS2 QDs and L-Cys-BODIPY molecules (Fig. 2B). The M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs appeared blue and green colors under the irradiation of a UV lamp (insets in Fig. 2A and B). Subsequently, we further evaluated the photostability of two kinds of QDs under continuous exposure of a 150 W Xe lamp (Fig. 2C). With reference to fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) that underwent rapid photobleaching, the fluorescence intensity of M − MoS2 QDs and BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs at 418 nm both slightly decreased (>94%) under continuous light irradiation for 3 h. Moreover, under the identical condition, the fluorescence of L-Cys-BODIPY in the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs decayed with irradiation time due to photo bleaching. The fluorescence intensity ratio from two emission wavelengths (F518 nm/F418 nm) of the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs was maintained almost unchanged in 10–120 mM NaCl, in the pH range of 2.0–12.0, and further this could be storage for 30 days with significant loss (Fig. 2D, E, and 2F). Moreover, the MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs. After incubating 0.3 mg mL−1 of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs with HeLa cells for 24 h, the cell viability remained above 90% (Fig. S3, supplementary material). These findings were signify that the biocompatible BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs should have great potential in cellular bio-imaging and biosensors.

Encouraged by the successful preparation of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs, we further explored the implementation of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs for ratiometric detection of biothiols, as illustrated in Fig. 3 A–C. According to the fact that L-Cys-BODIPY has a low molar absorption coefficient (7 × 10−10 M−1 cm−1) at 340 nm (curve a in Fig. 4 A), the M − MoS2 QDs in the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs still exhibit an intense blue emission at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm due to a small IFE (Fig. 3B). Meanwhile, L-Cys-BODIPY molecules on the surface of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs emit weak fluorescence owing to their π-π stacking between each BODIPY moieties. As long as biothiols are present in the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs, weakly fluorescent of L-Cys-BODIPY molecules that are converted to strongly fluorescent of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules through their thiol-disulfide reaction (Fig. 3C). Subsequently, the produced disulfide molecules preferably bind to the surface of M − MoS2 QDs [39]. Meanwhile, the liberated L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY can quench their fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs via IFE process [43–45] since their absorption spectrum (molar absorption coefficient of 8 × 10−8 M−1 cm−1) overlaps with the excitation wavelength of M − MoS2 QDs (curve b in Fig. 4A). Consequently, the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs probe can be employed for ratiometric detection of biothiols by monitoring the ratio of maximal fluorescence intensity of L-Cys-BODIPY at 518 nm to that of M − MoS2 QDs at 418 nm. In confirmation of the sensing mechanism mentioned above, we incubated the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs with different concentrations of GSH, homocysteine, and cysteine in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) for 1 h. As the GSH concentration varied from 1 to 10 mM in the proposed probe, the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs progressively decayed together with a gradual increase in the fluorescence of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY (Fig. 5 A). The BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs provided a similar fluorescence response toward a micromolar concentration of cysteine and homocysteine (Fig. 5B and C). Evidently, the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs are well-suited for ratiometric quantification of biothiols via the combination of IFE process [43–46] and also thiol-disulfide reaction. To further support this IFE process between L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY and M − MoS2 QDs, we measured the fluorescence lifetimes and spectra of M − MoS2 QDs as a function of the concentration of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. With varying the concentration of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY from 0 to 30 μM, the fluorescence lifetime of M − MoS2 QDs kept almost constant (Fig. 4B). Likewise, little-to-no change in the fluorescence lifetime was observable in the previously reported IFE process between tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid and black phosphorus quantum dots [45], as well as hydrolyzed p-nitrophenylphosphate and 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid-capped gold nanoclusters [47]. In other words, photoinduced electron transfer or Förster resonance energy transfer process rarely occurs between the M − MoS2 QDs and L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules. However, the fluorescence of M − MoS2 QDs was gradually quenched with increasing the concentration of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY (Fig. 4C). It is clear that a higher concentration of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules can absorbs with more excitation light, as a resulting more efficient IFE of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules were attached on the surface of M − MoS2 QDs.

Plotting the values of F518 nm/F418 nm against the concentrations of GSH, homocysteine, and cysteine generated three linear calibration curves for the quantification of 1–10 mM GSH, 1–10 μM homocysteine, and 1–10 μM cysteine, in sequence (Fig. 5D, E, and 5F). The limits of detection (LODs) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 were 0.3 mM, 0.3 μM, and 0.3 μM for GSH, homocysteine, and cysteine, respectively. Since the concentration of cysteine (150–250 μM) in human plasma was reported to be approximately 30-fold higher than that of homocysteine [3], the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs could be applicable for the quantitative determination of plasma cysteine without the interference of homocysteine. Additionally, the sensing range of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs probe was suggested to be well-suited for imaging, further changes in the intracellular GSH level that ranges from 1 to 10 mM. It’s interesting to clarify that, the dependence on ratiometric response of the proposed probe with different type of biothiols. Given that the zeta potential of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs was measured to be −4.0 mV in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4), they could repel with negatively charged GSH molecules (pKa1 = 2.1, pKa2 = 3.5, pKa3 = 8.7, and pKa4 = 9.2). Under the same buffer condition, homocysteine (pKa1 = 2.2, pKa2 = 8.7, and pKa3 = 10.8) and cysteine (pKa1 = 2.1, pKa2 = 8.2, and pKa3 = 10.3) these two biothiols own less negative charges and smaller molecular size than GSH. Therefore, in contrast to GSH, homocysteine, and cysteine might be easily collide with the surface of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs, facilitating to thiol-induced reduction of L-Cys-BODIPY to L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY. The selectivity of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs was evaluated by substituting of biothiols along with other amino acids which includes adenosine triphosphate, potassium ions, glucose, and cysteamine. Fig. S4 (supplementary material) shows that the proposed probe was only highly responsive toward GSH, homocysteine, and cysteine. These findings are indicate that the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs offer satisfactory sensitivity and good selectivity to the intracellular GSH and plasma cysteine.

Ratiometric sensing of biothiols the-as developed BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs probe encourages us to detect thiol product-related enzymatic reactions that includes SAHH-mediated cleavage of SAH [48,49] and glutathione reductase-catalyzed hydrolysis of GSH disulfide (Fig. S5, supplementary material). After a mixture of 0–900 units L−1 SAHH and 100 μM SAH was incubated with BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs, the catalytically produced homocysteine further triggered an increase in the fluorescence of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY and a reduction in the fluorescence from M − MoS2 QDs (Fig. S6, supplementary material). Under the same incubation condition, the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs provided a similar response toward the products (i.e., GSH) from the reaction of 0–850 units L−1 glutathione reductase and 10 mM GSH disulfide (Fig. S7, supplementary material). The F518 nm/F418 nm value of the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs was linearly responsive to an increase in the concentration of SAHH from 1 to 900 units L−1 (inset in Fig. S6, supplementary material) and also the concentration of glutathione reductase from 1 to 850 units L−1 (inset in Fig. S7, supplementary material). The proposed probe would provide LODs of SAHH and glutathione reductase corresponding to 0.3 and 0.3 units L−1, respectively. The sensitivity of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs in the detection of SAHH activity is superior to that of the previously reported methods, which including of thymine-based molecular beacons (LOD of 4 units L−1) [48] and fluorosurfactant-stabilized gold nanoparticles (minimum detectable concentration of 100 units L−1 [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric imaging of intracellular GSH level
ce:section.ce:para: We further examined the practicability of the proposed probe for ratiometric imaging of intracellular GSH levels. Notably, the mean concentration of intracellular GSH in HeLa cells was demonstrated to be around 5 mM [50]. The incubation of HeLa cells with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs was conducted for 2 h, followed by the visualization of their fluorescent images with CLSM. Fig. 6 A and B shows that the labeling of HeLa cells with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs elicited clear intracellular blue and green fluorescence that arise from the M − MoS2 QDs and L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY, respectively. These result reflects that the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs are indeed permeable to the cell membrane and then react with the intracellular GSH, resulting in the production of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY from the proposed probe. The BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs probe were further used to monitor GSH-related reactions in HeLa cells. HeLa cells were treated with either 10 mM L-BSO or 1 mM GSH monoester (GSH-OET) before the labeling of the cells with the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs. In comparison to non-treated cells, the cytoplasm of L-BSO-treated cells displayed a relatively bright fluorescence in the blue channel together with a relatively weak fluorescence in the green channel (Fig. 6E and F). Since L-BSO can easily enter into the cellular cytoplasm and then impede the synthesis of intracellular GSH [51], the resulting of decreased GSH levels in their less liberation of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY from the proposed probe. In other words, a small IFE of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules onto the surface of M − MoS2 QDs occurs in the cytoplasm of the cells. Considering that GSH-OET can readily transport to the cell and be able to hydrolyzed to GSH inside the cytoplasm [24], GSH-OET-treated cells contained a higher concentration of GSH than non-treated cells. As a result, the cytoplasm of GSH-OET-treated cells are revealed a stronger green fluorescence and a weaker blue fluorescence than that of non-treated cells (Fig. 6I and J). The merge images of non-, L-BSO- and GSH-OET-treated cells displayed dark blue, bright blue, and mint-green color, in sequence (Fig. 6C, G, and 6K). The ratio of mean fluorescence from L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY (Fgreen) as compared to M − MoS2 QDs (Fblue) inside the cells reflects their level of intracellular GSH in non-, L-BSO- and GSH-OET-treated cells (Fig. 6D, H, and 6L). These findings are clearly point out that the BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs could be powerful for the quantitative determination of GSH level in a single cell.

This study has developed the dual-emission of BODPIY-M-MoS2 QDs probe for ratiometric sensing of biothiols, intracellular GSH-related reactions, and thiol product-related enzymatic reactions. The sensing mechanism of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs probe is guided by two concepts, including (i) the IFE-mediated fluorescence quenching of the M − MoS2 QDs by L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules and (ii) the thiol-induced reduction of weakly fluorescent L-Cys-BODIPY molecules could easily converted to strongly fluorescent of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY molecules through the thiol-disulfide reaction. The presence of various concentrations of L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY could rarely influenced by fluorescence lifetime of M − MoS2 QDs, confirming that, the IFE process between L-cysteine-conjugated BODIPY and M − MoS2 QDs. In contrast to, dual-emission probe of the-as developed BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs would allow their accurate and precise quantification of 1–10 mM GSH, 1–10 μM cysteine, and 1–10 μM homocysteine with LODs of 300, 0.3, and 0.3 μM, respectively. As a first example, the proposed probe combined with the enzymatic reaction was capable of ratiometrically quantifying of 1–900 units L−1 SAHH and 1–850 units L−1 glutathione reductase. The sensitivity of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs for probing thiol product-related enzymatic reactions is superior to that of previously reported sensors [48,49]. More importantly, the responsive range of BODIPY-M-MoS2 QDs toward GSH has been shown to be successful for ratiometrically monitoring the change in the intracellular GSH level. We clearly point out that the direct physisorption of L-Cys-BODIPY on the surface of M − MoS2 QDs might be open up for new possibilities of fabricating different organic dye-adsorbed M − MoS2 QDs as a ratiometric sensor. For example, it is expected that thiolated fluorescein-modified M − MoS2 QDs could be implemented for ratiometric sensing of intracellular pH change.

Yzu-Yun Huang: Investigation. Wei-Lung Tseng: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
